title: What Is a Phase I Archaeological Survey?
content:
A Phase I archaeological survey is the first step in identifying whether a project area contains culturally significant sites that may be protected under federal and state law. Before ground is broken on a road expansion, pipeline corridor, or residential development, federal and state regulations often require that a qualified archaeologist evaluate the land. At Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research (TVAR), our RPA-certified archaeologists conduct Phase I surveys across the Southeast United States, helping developers, agencies, and landowners understand what lies beneath the surface—and what obligations they may have before construction begins.

Why Phase I Surveys Are Required

Federal law is the primary driver behind Phase I archaeological surveys. Section 106 of the National Historic Preservation Act (NHPA) requires federal agencies to consider the effects of their undertakings on historic properties, which includes archaeological sites. Any project that involves a federal permit, federal funding, or federal land is subject to this review. State-level environmental review processes often carry similar requirements.

The goal of Section 106 is not to stop development—it is to ensure that decision-makers have accurate information about cultural resources before irreversible actions are taken. A Phase I survey gives agencies and project proponents that information. If significant resources are identified, the project team can adjust plans, avoid sensitive areas, or advance to a Phase II evaluation to learn more. Without a Phase I survey, developers risk project delays, legal challenges, or costly retroactive mitigation if sites are discovered during construction.

In Alabama and across the Southeast, the State Historic Preservation Office (SHPO) coordinates with federal agencies to ensure that surveys meet professional standards. TVAR works directly with SHPO and federal clients to produce reports that satisfy regulatory review and move projects forward efficiently.

What Happens During a Phase I Survey

A Phase I archaeological survey combines archival research with systematic fieldwork. The process typically begins with a background study—sometimes called a records search or literature review—in which archaeologists examine historical maps, previously recorded site files, deed records, and ethnographic sources to identify areas of high sensitivity within the project area. This research informs where fieldwork should be concentrated.

In the field, archaeologists conduct pedestrian survey, walking systematic transects across the project area to look for surface artifacts, soil anomalies, and other indicators of past human activity. In areas where ground visibility is limited by vegetation, shovel test pits (STPs) are excavated at regular intervals—typically every 15 to 30 meters—to sample the subsurface. Each STP is screened through wire mesh to recover small artifacts such as lithic flakes, ceramic sherds, or faunal remains.

When artifacts or features are encountered, archaeologists record their location using GIS technology, photograph the context, and collect representative samples. All findings are documented in standardized field forms that will support the final report. Our team at TVAR uses rigorous field methods that meet or exceed the Secretary of the Interior's Standards for Archaeological Documentation, ensuring that our work holds up under agency and SHPO review.

Deliverables: The Phase I Survey Report

At the conclusion of fieldwork, the project archaeologist compiles a Phase I survey report. This document is the primary deliverable and serves as the official record of what was found—or not found—within the area of potential effect (APE). A complete Phase I report typically includes a project description and regulatory context, a summary of background research, a detailed methods section, site forms for any newly identified archaeological sites, and recommendations for further action.

Recommendations are perhaps the most consequential part of the report. If no sites were identified, the archaeologist will typically recommend a finding of no historic properties affected, allowing the project to proceed. If sites were identified, the report will assess their potential significance and recommend either a Phase II evaluation to determine National Register eligibility or, in some cases, avoidance of the site entirely.

TVAR delivers clear, well-organized reports that agencies can act on quickly. We understand that project schedules are real constraints, and we work to produce technically sound documents without unnecessary delays. Our reports reflect 14-plus years of experience navigating SHPO and federal agency review processes throughout Alabama and the broader Southeast.

How TVAR Approaches Phase I Surveys

Tennessee Valley Archaeological Research brings specialized expertise to every Phase I project. As a women-owned firm with RPA-certified archaeologists on staff, we combine professional rigor with a practical understanding of the development and regulatory landscape. Our team has conducted Phase I surveys for transportation projects, utility corridors, residential and commercial developments, and federal facility expansions across Alabama and neighboring states.

We tailor our survey designs to the specific characteristics of each project area. Archaeological sensitivity varies enormously across the landscape—a floodplain terrace adjacent to a creek carries very different potential than a heavily disturbed industrial site—and our methods reflect that variability. We use GIS and remote sensing tools, including aerial imagery analysis and LiDAR data interpretation, to refine our field strategies and ensure that high-sensitivity zones receive appropriate attention.

TVAR also emphasizes clear communication throughout the process. We keep clients informed at each stage, flag potential issues early, and coordinate proactively with regulatory agencies to avoid surprises. Our goal is to help you meet your legal obligations efficiently so that your project can move forward with confidence.

Start Your Phase I Survey with TVAR

If your project requires a Phase I archaeological survey in Alabama or the Southeast, TVAR is ready to help. Our experienced team will guide you through the regulatory process, design an appropriate survey, and deliver a report that meets agency standards. Early consultation is the best way to keep your project on schedule and avoid costly surprises down the road.

Contact TVAR today to discuss your project needs and get a proposal for Phase I archaeological survey services.
excerpt: Learn what a Phase I archaeological survey involves, why it's required, and how TVAR's RPA-certified archaeologists conduct surveys across the Southeast.
